document_title: AIADMK Election Manifesto - Legislative Assembly General Election 2026
extraction_scope: Only numbered manifesto points were included. Non-point narrative text such as preamble and conclusion was excluded.
hierarchy_convention: Each point includes hierarchy_down_to_top ordered from the point itself upward to subsection/section/document.

points.text: Special assistance of Rs. 10,000/- to every family to manage the tax burden and price rise: Under the administratively inefficient Vidiya DMK government led by Stalin, the prices of essential commodities have skyrocketed like poison over the past five years. Consequently, the public has been severely affected. Similarly, various taxes such as property tax, house tax, electricity charges, and water tax have been hiked. As a result, the daily financial burden on families has multiplied. Therefore, to reduce this burden on the people, a special assistance of Rs. 10,000/- will be provided to every family.
points.title: Special assistance of Rs. 10,000/- to every family to manage the tax burden and price rise

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: Special assistance of Rs. 10,000/- to every family to manage the tax burden and price rise
AIADMK Election Manifesto - Legislative Assembly General Election 2026

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu Budget 2024-25 Highlights
evidence.snippet: The state provides various welfare schemes like the Pongal gift hamper (usually Rs 1,000) and monthly assistance, but no universal Rs 10,000 one-time tax relief scheme exists.

plan_existence.notes: While the TN government provides periodic cash gifts (e.g., Rs. 1,000 for Pongal) and monthly assistance for women, a universal one-time transfer of Rs. 10,000 to all families for price rise is a new proposal.

fiscal.comments: Tamil Nadu has approximately 2.24 crore ration cards. A transfer of Rs. 10,000 per family would cost approximately Rs. 22,400 crore. This represents nearly 7-8% of the state's total revenue receipts, posing a massive fiscal challenge given the state's existing debt levels.

legal.comments: Executive power allows for the disbursement of welfare grants; no significant legal or constitutional hurdles.

administrative.comments: The state has a robust Direct Benefit Transfer (DBT) mechanism linked to PDS/Ration card data, making distribution feasible.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented within the first year of a term as a one-time payout.

political.comments: Highly popular move likely to receive significant public support during economic inflation.

feasibility.overall_comments: The scheme is administratively simple but fiscally reckless. The primary risk is the extreme strain on the state exchequer, potentially leading to cuts in capital expenditure.
key_risks: Extreme fiscal deficit increase
Inflationary pressure due to sudden liquidity injection
Diversion of funds from infrastructure projects

points.text: 'Kula Vilakku Scheme' to protect women's welfare: To create economic equality in society, a monthly assistance of Rs. 2,000/- will be provided to all family cardholders through the Kula Vilakku Scheme. This amount will be deposited directly into the bank account of the female head of the family.
points.title: 'Kula Vilakku Scheme' to protect women's welfare

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: 'Kula Vilakku Scheme' to protect women's welfare
AIADMK Election Manifesto - Legislative Assembly General Election 2026

evidence.title: Kalaignar Magalir Urimai Thogai Thittam - Official Site
evidence.snippet: The current scheme provides Rs. 1,000 per month to eligible female heads of households.

plan_existence.notes: The proposal doubles the existing benefit from Rs. 1,000 to Rs. 2,000 and implies a shift from 'eligible' cardholders to 'all' cardholders, representing a significant expansion.

fiscal.comments: The current KMUT scheme costs ~Rs. 12,000 crore annually for 1.15 crore beneficiaries. Expanding this to all 2.24 crore cardholders at Rs. 2,000/month would cost ~Rs. 53,760 crore annually, which is nearly 20% of the state's revenue receipts.

legal.comments: Welfare schemes for women are protected under Directive Principles and state policy.

administrative.comments: The infrastructure for KMUT is already in place; expanding the database and increasing the transfer amount is administratively straightforward.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented immediately by amending the existing KMUT government order.

political.comments: Direct cash transfers to women are highly effective political tools with high voter resonance.

feasibility.overall_comments: While politically and administratively sound, the fiscal burden is unsustainable without massive revenue generation or central assistance.
key_risks: Unsustainable recurring expenditure
Potential for exclusion errors if 'all' is later restricted
Crowding out of other social sector spending

points.text: Free bus travel scheme for men, similar to women: A free bus travel scheme will be implemented for men traveling in city buses. Furthermore, the existing free bus travel scheme for women in city buses will continue to be implemented.
points.title: Free bus travel scheme for men, similar to women

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: Free bus travel scheme for men, similar to women
AIADMK Election Manifesto - Legislative Assembly General Election 2026

evidence.title: TNSTC Free Travel for Women
evidence.snippet: The government currently provides free travel for women, transgender persons, and persons with disabilities in ordinary town buses.

plan_existence.notes: The current scheme specifically excludes men. This proposal expands the existing 'Vidiyal Payanam' model to include the male population.

fiscal.comments: TNSTC (Tamil Nadu State Transport Corporation) is already facing heavy losses. The women's free travel scheme costs the state ~Rs. 2,800 crore in subsidies annually. Including men would effectively eliminate fare revenue from ordinary town buses, requiring a total bailout of the transport corporations.

legal.comments: No legal barriers to providing free public services.

administrative.comments: Easy to implement as it simply involves not charging for tickets, though monitoring passenger load becomes harder.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented via a single government order to transport corporations.

political.comments: Popular among the working class, but may face criticism regarding the financial health of public utilities.

feasibility.overall_comments: The scheme would likely bankrupt the state transport corporations unless the government provides 100% operational subsidy, which is fiscally daunting.
key_risks: Bankruptcy of TNSTC
Deterioration of bus quality and maintenance due to lack of revenue
Overcrowding in ordinary buses leading to safety issues

points.text: 'Amma Illam Scheme' providing houses for homeless families: Through the "Amma Illam Scheme," the government itself will purchase land and build free concrete houses for those in rural areas who do not own a house plot or a house. Similarly, for the homeless poor in urban areas, multi-story apartments will be built and provided free of cost through the "Amma Illam Scheme." Under this scheme, when Scheduled Caste people living in a joint family, or their sons/daughters get married and establish a separate household, they will be given priority and provided with constructed houses.
points.title: 'Amma Illam Scheme' providing houses for homeless families

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: 'Amma Illam Scheme' providing houses for homeless families
AIADMK Election Manifesto - Legislative Assembly General Election 2026

existing_scheme_names: Tamil Nadu Urban Habitat Development Board (TNUHDB) projects

evidence.title: Kalaignarin Kanavu Illam Scheme Launch
evidence.snippet: The TN government launched a scheme to build 8 lakh houses in rural areas by 2030.

plan_existence.notes: The 'Amma Illam' proposal mirrors existing state and central housing schemes but adds a specific priority for newly married SC individuals and emphasizes government-purchased land for the landless.

fiscal.comments: Building houses for all homeless is extremely capital intensive. The current state budget for housing is already stretched. Purchasing land for the landless (as promised) adds a massive cost layer that current schemes usually avoid by requiring the beneficiary to own land.

legal.comments: Land acquisition for housing is legally permissible but often faces litigation and delays.

administrative.comments: The TNUHDB and Rural Development departments have the capacity, but the scale of 'all homeless' is a massive logistical challenge.

timeline.comments: Housing projects, especially multi-story urban apartments, typically take 3-5 years to complete, making universal coverage within one term unlikely.

political.comments: Housing is a high-impact political promise with long-term visibility.

feasibility.overall_comments: A standard but ambitious housing promise. The specific commitment to purchase land for the landless is the most difficult and expensive part to fulfill.
key_risks: High cost of land acquisition
Construction delays and quality control issues
Identification of genuine beneficiaries in urban areas

points.text: 100 days employment scheme will be increased to 150 days: The Central Government has announced that the 100 days employment scheme will be increased to 125 days. Steps will be taken to implement this rural development scheme to provide up to 150 days of employment.
points.title: 100 days employment scheme will be increased to 150 days

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: 100 days employment scheme will be increased to 150 days
AIADMK Election Manifesto - Legislative Assembly General Election 2026

existing_scheme_names: Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee Scheme (MGNREGS)
Viksit Bharat – Guarantee for Rozgar and Ajeevika Mission (Gramin)

evidence.title: G RAM G to replace NREGA, guarantee 125 days of work
evidence.snippet: The Union government proposed the VB-G RAM G Bill in December 2025 to replace MGNREGA, increasing the statutory guarantee from 100 to 125 days.

evidence.title: MGNREGS - Rural Development & Panchayat Raj Department
evidence.snippet: The scheme currently guarantees 100 days of employment. Tamil Nadu has previously increased this to 150 days during drought years using state funds.

plan_existence.notes: The Central Government has moved to increase the baseline to 125 days under the new VB-G RAM G Act (2025). The promise to provide 150 days would require the Tamil Nadu state government to fund the additional 25 days entirely from the state exchequer.

fiscal.comments: Tamil Nadu has ~88.57 lakh active workers. Increasing from 125 to 150 days (25 extra days) at a wage of ~Rs. 300/day would cost roughly Rs. 6,600 crore annually if all active households utilize the full quota. This is a significant but manageable burden for a state with TN's welfare budget.

legal.comments: States have the statutory right to provide additional days of work beyond the Central guarantee using their own funds, as seen in previous drought-relief measures.

administrative.comments: The administrative machinery for MGNREGA is well-established in Tamil Nadu, making the rollout of additional days seamless.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented immediately via a Government Order (GO) at the start of the financial year.

political.comments: High public reception in rural areas; aligns with the state's historical focus on rural employment.

feasibility.overall_comments: Highly feasible as it builds on existing infrastructure. The primary challenge is the fiscal cost of funding the 25-day gap between the Central guarantee (125) and the state promise (150).
key_risks: Fiscal strain on state budget
Delayed wage payments if state funds are not released timely
Potential reduction in Central funding if the new 60:40 ratio is strictly enforced

AIADMK Election Manifesto - Legislative Assembly General Election 2026

evidence.title: Amma Two Wheeler Scheme for Working Women
evidence.snippet: In the year 2021, the DMK government scrapped the Amma Two Wheeler scheme and commenced the 'Zero Ticket Bus Travel Scheme for Women'.

plan_existence.notes: The scheme was a flagship of the previous AIADMK government and was discontinued in 2021. Reintroducing it for 5 lakh women represents a return to a direct-subsidy model for private transport.

fiscal.comments: A subsidy of Rs. 25,000 for 5 lakh women equals a total outlay of Rs. 1,250 crore. While specific, this is a one-time cost per beneficiary but a large aggregate hit to the budget.

legal.comments: No legal hurdles; the state has full authority to provide subsidies for vehicle purchases.

administrative.comments: The TN Corporation for Development of Women (TNCDW) has prior experience implementing this exact scheme, so the delivery mechanism is ready.

timeline.comments: Can be delivered within a 5-year term, likely in phases of 1 lakh women per year.

political.comments: Popular among working women, though it may be criticized as a reversal of the current 'free bus' policy.

feasibility.overall_comments: Highly feasible administratively due to past implementation. The fiscal cost is clearly defined and manageable within the state's welfare budget.
key_risks: Selection bias/corruption in beneficiary identification
Inflation in two-wheeler prices reducing the impact of the fixed subsidy
Potential overlap or conflict with green energy/EV transition goals

points.text: Pensions for the elderly and others increased to Rs. 2,000/-: The monthly social security pension provided to the elderly, widows, destitute unmarried women, deserted women, differently-abled persons, and transgender persons under the social pension scheme will be increased from Rs. 1,200/- to Rs. 2,000/-.
points.title: Pensions for the elderly and others increased to Rs. 2,000/

AIADMK Election Manifesto - Legislative Assembly General Election 2026

existing_scheme_names: Indira Gandhi National Old Age Pension Scheme

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu cabinet decides to enhance social security pension to Rs 1200
evidence.snippet: In July 2023, the TN government increased the monthly pension from Rs. 1,000 to Rs. 1,200 for 30.55 lakh beneficiaries.

plan_existence.notes: The current standard pension is Rs. 1,200. The promise seeks to increase this by 66% to Rs. 2,000. Disabled persons already receive Rs. 1,500 in some categories.

fiscal.comments: Increasing the pension by Rs. 800 (from 1,200 to 2,000) for ~30.55 lakh beneficiaries would cost an additional ~Rs. 2,932 crore per year. This is a recurring expenditure that adds significant long-term pressure on the state's revenue deficit.

legal.comments: Social security is a state subject; the government has full authority to set pension rates.

administrative.comments: Direct Benefit Transfer (DBT) systems are already in place for these beneficiaries.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented immediately via executive order.

political.comments: Extremely high political appeal; social security pensions are a core component of the Dravidian welfare model.

feasibility.overall_comments: Technically and administratively simple, but fiscally demanding. The recurring nature of the cost is the only major hurdle.
key_risks: Increasing revenue deficit
Exclusion of eligible applicants due to budget caps
Inflation eroding the real value of the hike

points.text: Waiver of education loans obtained from banks: To protect the welfare of students and parents, the government will take over and waive the education loans of those who borrowed from banks but are unable to pay their EMIs due to unemployment.
points.title: Waiver of education loans obtained from banks

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: Waiver of education loans obtained from banks
AIADMK Election Manifesto - Legislative Assembly General Election 2026

evidence.title: T.N. waives educational loans worth ₹48.95 crore
evidence.snippet: In February 2025, the TN government waived Rs. 48.95 crore in loans for SC/ST students for loans taken between 1972 and 2010.

evidence.title: 49cr in edu loans of dalits waived
evidence.snippet: Critics noted that while Rs. 49 crore was waived, the total pending education loan in the state was approximately Rs. 16,302 crore.

plan_existence.notes: Existing waivers are highly targeted (SC/ST) and cover very old loans. A blanket waiver for all 'unemployed' borrowers is a massive expansion of scope.

fiscal.comments: With total pending education loans in TN estimated at over Rs. 16,000 crore, a general waiver for the unemployed would likely cost several thousand crores. The state would have to pay banks the principal and interest, which is a massive fiscal drain.

legal.comments: Commercial banks are regulated by the RBI and the Central Government. A state cannot unilaterally 'waive' bank loans; it must 'take over' the debt by paying the banks, which requires complex tripartite agreements.

administrative.comments: Defining and verifying 'unemployment' for the purpose of a loan waiver is administratively difficult and prone to fraud/misrepresentation.

timeline.comments: Negotiations with banks and verification of lakhs of applicants would take 2-3 years.

political.comments: Very popular among the youth and middle class, but risks backlash from those who already paid their loans.

feasibility.overall_comments: Fiscally and legally precarious. While politically attractive, the sheer volume of debt (Rs. 16,000cr+) and the difficulty of verifying unemployment status make this promise nearly impossible to implement fully.
key_risks: Extreme fiscal burden
Moral hazard (encouraging future defaults)
Legal disputes with nationalized banks
Difficulty in defining 'unemployment' criteria

points.text: 3 free gas cylinders per year as livelihood assistance: As livelihood assistance, 3 free gas cylinders will be provided annually to the family of Rice Cardholders.
points.title: 3 free gas cylinders per year as livelihood assistance

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: 3 free gas cylinders per year as livelihood assistance
AIADMK Election Manifesto - Legislative Assembly General Election 2026

evidence.title: Implementation of the Deepam Scheme in 2024 - 2025
evidence.snippet: Each eligible beneficiary will receive three free LPG cylinders per year. The beneficiary must possess a valid rice card issued by the state government (Andhra Pradesh).

evidence.title: PM Ujjwala Yojana - LiveChennai
evidence.snippet: Under this scheme - Pradhan Mantri Ujjwala Yojana – free PG cooking gas cylinders are being distributed to the citizens living Below Poverty Line (BP).

plan_existence.notes: While the Central Government's Ujjwala scheme provides initial connections and occasional relief, a permanent state-funded scheme for 3 free refills annually does not exist in Tamil Nadu. A similar model was recently launched in Andhra Pradesh (Deepam 2.0).

fiscal.comments: With approximately 2.2 crore rice cardholders in Tamil Nadu, providing 3 cylinders at a market rate of ~Rs. 800-900 each would cost the state exchequer over Rs. 5,300 crore annually, significantly straining the fiscal deficit.

legal.comments: States have the authority to provide welfare subsidies and essential commodities under the Directive Principles of State Policy.

administrative.comments: The existing Public Distribution System (PDS) and Aadhaar-linked LPG databases make identification and Direct Benefit Transfer (DBT) highly feasible.

timeline.comments: Can be rolled out within the first year of governance using existing oil marketing company (OMC) networks.

political.comments: High populist appeal; directly addresses inflation and cost-of-living concerns for the largest voting bloc (low-income families).

feasibility.overall_comments: Highly popular but fiscally reckless. While administratively simple to implement via DBT, the massive recurring cost is the primary hurdle for a state already facing high debt.
key_risks: Massive fiscal burden on state budget
Potential for leakage if Aadhaar-seeding is incomplete
Dependence on central oil marketing companies for supply

points.text: • Rs. 10 lakh compensation for families of Jallikattu tamers: If Jallikattu tamers unfortunately lose their lives while taming bulls during the traditional Tamil martial sport of Jallikattu, their families will be provided with Rs. 10 lakh. Similarly, Rs. 2 lakh will be provided for medical treatment to those who sustain severe injuries. • A Government subsidy of Rs. 5 lakh will be provided to enthusiastically organize Jallikattu events in places where government orders have been issued. • The online registration system currently followed for organizing Jallikattu events will be cancelled. The previously existing manual system will be reinstated.
points.title: Rs. 10 lakh compensation for families of Jallikattu tamers
bullets: Rs. 10 lakh compensation for families of Jallikattu tamers: If Jallikattu tamers unfortunately lose their lives while taming bulls during the traditional Tamil martial sport of Jallikattu, their families will be provided with Rs. 10 lakh. Similarly, Rs. 2 lakh will be provided for medical treatment to those who sustain severe injuries.
A Government subsidy of Rs. 5 lakh will be provided to enthusiastically organize Jallikattu events in places where government orders have been issued.
The online registration system currently followed for organizing Jallikattu events will be cancelled. The previously existing manual system will be reinstated.

hierarchy_down_to_top.label: Rs. 10 lakh compensation for families of Jallikattu tamers
AIADMK Election Manifesto - Legislative Assembly General Election 2026

existing_scheme_names: Chief Minister's Public Relief Fund (CMPRF) - Jallikattu Solatium

evidence.title: CM Stalin announces Rs 3 lakh to families of 2 men killed in Jallikattu events
evidence.snippet: Chief Minister MK Stalin has announced a solatium of Rs 3 lakh to the families of the deceased.

evidence.title: T.N. government announces relaxations in norms for jallikattu organisers
evidence.snippet: The State government has decided to do away with the online registration of participants for jallikattu... registration will henceforth be handled by the respective district administrations.

plan_existence.notes: The current compensation is Rs. 3 lakh; the promise seeks to more than triple it. Notably, the current government already moved away from online registration to district-level manual/local registration in early 2026.

fiscal.comments: The number of fatalities in Jallikattu is relatively low (typically under 20 per year), making the total annual outlay for compensation and subsidies (estimated < Rs. 20 crore) very manageable.

legal.comments: Jallikattu is legally protected under the Tamil Nadu Amendment Act, 2017. Compensation and subsidies are within state powers.

administrative.comments: Reverting to a manual registration system may reduce transparency and increase the risk of corruption or safety protocol bypasses compared to digital tracking.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented immediately via executive order.

political.comments: Strong cultural resonance in rural Tamil Nadu, particularly in the 'Jallikattu belt' (southern districts).

feasibility.overall_comments: A low-cost, high-impact cultural promise. The increase in compensation is feasible, though the reversal of digital registration is a regressive administrative step.
key_risks: Administrative opacity with manual registration
Potential for fraudulent injury claims
Safety risks if manual systems fail to vet participants properly

AIADMK Election Manifesto - Legislative Assembly General Election 2026

existing_scheme_names: TAMCO Term Loan / Micro Credit Schemes

evidence.title: A BRIEF NOTE ON TAMCO - Government of Tamil Nadu
evidence.snippet: The Objective of TAMCO is to provide loans at low rate of interest to Minorities... Rate of Interest: Female Beneficiaries - 6% p.a.

plan_existence.notes: TAMCO currently provides loans to minority women at 6% interest. The promise to make them 'interest-free' is an amendment to the existing interest structure.

fiscal.comments: The cost involves the state absorbing the 6% interest currently charged. Given the typical loan volumes of TAMCO (approx. Rs. 5-10 crore per district), the total interest subvention cost would be manageable.

legal.comments: State corporations like TAMCO have the mandate to set interest rates and provide subsidies for specific target groups.

administrative.comments: TAMCO has an established infrastructure for loan disbursement and recovery through cooperative banks.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented within 6 months by modifying TAMCO's lending guidelines.

political.comments: Appeals to minority vote banks and women; however, may face criticism regarding 'appeasement' from opposition parties.

feasibility.overall_comments: A highly feasible expansion of an existing successful scheme. The fiscal impact is low as it only requires interest subvention rather than full capital funding.
key_risks: Potential for higher default rates if 'interest-free' is perceived as a grant
Limited reach if TAMCO's capital base is not expanded
Political polarization over targeted minority benefits

plan_existence.notes: While low-interest loans and subsidies (up to 33%) exist for the differently-abled in cooperative banks, a blanket waiver of all outstanding loans is a new proposal.

fiscal.comments: Total outstanding loan amount for this specific category in cooperative banks is not publicly available in the provided sources.

legal.comments: The state government has full jurisdiction over cooperative banks and can authorize loan waivers, as seen with previous farm and jewel loan waivers.

administrative.comments: Cooperative banks have clear records of borrowers; identifying and waiving these specific accounts is administratively straightforward.

timeline.comments: Can be executed via a single Government Order (GO) within the first 100 days.

Exclusion of those who borrowed from nationalized/private banks

plan_existence.notes: The existing scheme provides significantly lower amounts (Rs. 600 for graduates vs the promised Rs. 2,000). The promise represents a 233% increase for graduates and a 150% increase for HSC holders.

legal.comments: No legal impediments; falls under state welfare powers.

administrative.comments: The Fisheries Department already has a verified database of beneficiaries and uses DBT.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented in the next fishing ban season (April-June) following the election.

political.comments: Strongly supported by the influential coastal fishing community.

feasibility.overall_comments: Highly feasible and realistic. The administrative machinery is mature, and the fiscal impact is relatively small compared to other state-wide subsidies.
key_risks: Inflation eroding the value of the increased relief
Demands for similar hikes in lean period relief (non-ban months)

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu CM launches Pongal gift hamper scheme for 2025
evidence.snippet: The Tamil Nadu government provided a Pongal gift hamper including 1 kg rice, 1 kg sugar, and a sugarcane, along with Rs. 1,000 cash for 2025.

plan_existence.notes: The promise to provide Rs. 1,000 cash along with the hamper is a continuation of the practice followed in 2024 and 2025.

fiscal.comments: With approximately 2.19 crore rice-drawing ration cards, the cash component alone costs roughly Rs. 2,190 crore annually. This is a significant recurring expenditure.

legal.comments: Standard welfare distribution via PDS; no legal issues.

administrative.comments: The PDS network and bank-linked accounts for DBT are fully operational and capable of handling this.

timeline.comments: Implemented annually in January.

political.comments: Extremely popular; any attempt to withdraw the cash component would face severe backlash.

feasibility.overall_comments: This is a low-risk, high-visibility promise that is already being implemented. The primary challenge is the annual fiscal burden on the state exchequer.
key_risks: High fiscal cost (over Rs. 2,000 crore) for a one-time annual gift
Logistical challenges in distributing physical hampers to 2 crore+ households

evidence.title: PM SVANidhi: Empowering Street Vendors
evidence.snippet: PM SVANidhi provides collateral-free working capital loans of up to ₹50,000 to street vendors, but it is a credit facility, not a waiver scheme.

plan_existence.notes: While the central government's PM SVANidhi provides loans to street vendors, there is currently no state or central scheme that waives these loans. Tamil Nadu has previously waived crop loans and SHG loans, but a specific waiver for urban street vendor loans from cooperative banks is a new proposal.

legal.comments: State governments have the authority to waive loans obtained from state-controlled cooperative banks.

administrative.comments: Identifying 'small' street vendors and verifying their pavement operations in urban areas requires rigorous field verification to prevent leakage.

timeline.comments: Loan waivers can be executed quickly through executive orders once the beneficiary list is finalized.

political.comments: Highly popular among the urban poor; however, it may face criticism for encouraging credit indiscipline in the cooperative banking sector.

feasibility.overall_comments: The scheme is legally sound but requires a clear definition of 'small street vendor' to be administratively viable. Fiscal impact cannot be assessed without data on the total outstanding loan volume in cooperative banks for this specific segment.
key_risks: Identification of genuine pavement vendors vs. permanent shop extensions
Potential for moral hazard in future cooperative lending
Administrative burden of verifying urban pavement operations

evidence.title: AIADMK announces free refrigerator to rice cardholders

plan_existence.notes: This is a new populist promise following the precedent of free fans, mixers, and grinders distributed by the AIADMK government in 2011. No such scheme currently exists in any Indian state.

fiscal.comments: With 2.22 crore rice cardholders, even a basic refrigerator costing ₹10,000 would require a one-time outlay of ₹22,200 crore. This is nearly 25% of the state's total social welfare budget for 2025-26.

legal.comments: State has the power to distribute consumer durables as welfare measures.

administrative.comments: Massive logistical challenge involving procurement, storage, and door-to-door delivery of bulky appliances to 22 million households.

timeline.comments: Given the scale and cost, distribution would likely take the entire 5-year term to complete, similar to the 2011 mixer-grinder scheme.

political.comments: High voter appeal, particularly among women, but may face criticism regarding state debt levels.

feasibility.overall_comments: While politically potent, the scheme is fiscally staggering and administratively daunting. The sheer volume of procurement and the resulting debt burden make it a high-risk promise.
key_risks: Extreme fiscal strain on state treasury
Procurement corruption and quality issues
Logistical failure in delivering bulky items to remote/rural areas
Environmental impact of mass disposal of old units

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu Civil Supplies Corporation - Special PDS

fiscal.comments: The current subsidy for Special PDS (dal and oil) is approximately Rs. 4,000-5,000 crore annually. Making these items entirely free would add a significant burden to the state's fiscal deficit, which was projected at over Rs. 90,000 crore for FY24-25.

legal.comments: The state has full authority to modify subsidy levels and distribution terms under the PDS framework.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented immediately via executive order as the supply chain is already active.

political.comments: Highly popular welfare measure with minimal political opposition, though it may face criticism regarding fiscal prudence.

key_risks: Increased fiscal deficit
Global commodity price volatility affecting procurement costs
Potential for leakage/black marketing of free goods

points.text: According to Perarignar Anna's principle, "We will extend our hand for friendship, and raise our voice for rights" is the guiding mantra of the All India Anna Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam. There is no denying that for India to shine as a strong superpower, the Central Government must be strong. At the same time, State Governments must also function independently with appropriate financial and administrative rights. Accordingly, it is our policy that the Central Government must provide proper financial and administrative devolution to the State Governments and this will pressed always.

evidence.title: Inter-State Council - Constitutional Provisions
evidence.snippet: Article 263 of the Constitution provides for the establishment of an Inter-State Council to inquire into and advise upon disputes between States and the Union.

plan_existence.notes: This is a political philosophy and policy stance regarding federalism rather than a specific welfare scheme. It aligns with long-standing Dravidian political ideology.

legal.comments: Financial and administrative devolution is governed by the Constitution (Articles 245-293). Significant changes require Central cooperation or Constitutional amendments.

timeline.comments: Achieving significant shifts in federal power dynamics is a long-term political process, unlikely to be fully realized within a single 5-year term without Central alignment.

political.comments: Strong resonance with the Tamil Nadu electorate; however, success depends entirely on the party's leverage with the Central Government.

feasibility.overall_comments: This is a statement of intent and political ideology. Its feasibility is tied to national political dynamics and the recommendations of the Finance Commission rather than state-level administrative action.
key_risks: Central government resistance
Constitutional limitations on state autonomy
Conflict with national-level fiscal consolidation goals

points.text: To protect the rights of the states, within the framework of the Indian Constitution, our stance is that along with the authoritative responsibilities granted to the states, the necessary financial allocations and responsibilities must be properly provided for the State Governments to function independently. Therefore, we will insist that additional financial devolution be provided to the states in accordance with changing circumstances.

evidence.title: 15th Finance Commission Report
evidence.snippet: The 15th Finance Commission recommended a vertical devolution of 41% from the divisible pool of taxes to the states.

plan_existence.notes: The promise is to advocate for higher devolution. Currently, the 16th Finance Commission is in the process of determining the next cycle of revenue sharing.

fiscal.comments: This promise aims to increase state revenue rather than spend it; however, the outcome is dependent on the Finance Commission, not the state budget.

legal.comments: The Finance Commission is a constitutional body (Article 280). States can only 'insist' or 'demand' via memoranda; they cannot unilaterally change devolution math.

administrative.comments: The state finance department is well-equipped to prepare and present technical arguments to the Finance Commission.

timeline.comments: The 16th Finance Commission's recommendations will cover the period from 2026-2031, aligning with the next government's term.

political.comments: A standard and popular demand for all regional parties in Tamil Nadu to ensure more resources for state-specific schemes.

feasibility.overall_comments: This is a diplomatic and political objective. While the state can effectively argue its case, the final decision rests with the Central Government and the Finance Commission's formula.
key_risks: Finance Commission criteria favoring less developed states
Cess and surcharges reducing the divisible pool
Political friction with the Union government

points.text: Pointing out that the Central Finance Commissions formed after the 12th Finance Commission have gradually reduced the financial allocation for Tamil Nadu, we continuously insist that financial devolution should be structured in a way that does not adversely affect developed states like Tamil Nadu. Standing firm on this stance, we will demand that Tamil Nadu receives its financial share without any reduction.

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu's memorandum to the 16th Finance Commission
evidence.snippet: The Tamil Nadu government has consistently argued that the state's share has declined from 6.637% in the 9th Finance Commission to 4.079% in the 15th Finance Commission.

plan_existence.notes: This is a specific grievance regarding the 'horizontal devolution' formula which penalizes states for better demographic and economic performance.

fiscal.comments: The goal is to prevent further revenue loss. Tamil Nadu has seen its share of the divisible pool shrink significantly over successive commissions.

legal.comments: The Terms of Reference (ToR) for the Finance Commission are set by the President. States have limited legal recourse if the formula prioritizes 'equity' over 'efficiency'.

administrative.comments: Tamil Nadu has a highly capable bureaucracy that consistently produces high-quality data to back these demands.

timeline.comments: The 16th Finance Commission is currently active; the window for influence is now and in the immediate future.

political.comments: This is a consensus issue in Tamil Nadu across party lines, as the state feels 'penalized for performing well'.

feasibility.overall_comments: A critical policy stance for the state's financial health. While the state's advocacy is strong, the outcome depends on a national-level formula that balances the needs of poorer states against the contributions of developed ones.
key_risks: Continued use of 2011 census data
Weightage given to 'income distance' criteria
Central government's fiscal space constraints

legal.comments: Delimitation is a constitutional process governed by the Union Government and the Delimitation Commission; a state can only advocate or pass resolutions but cannot legally block it alone.

administrative.comments: The act of 'insisting' or passing resolutions is well within the state's administrative and political capacity.

timeline.comments: The delimitation process is expected after the first census post-2026; advocacy can be continuous throughout the 5-year term.

political.comments: There is near-unanimous political consensus within Tamil Nadu across party lines to protect the state's representation.

feasibility.overall_comments: While the state has high political will and administrative ease in making this demand, the ultimate legal authority rests with the Union Government, making the actual outcome dependent on central cooperation.
key_risks: Constitutional mandate for population-based redrawing
Lack of veto power for individual states
Divergent interests of high-population states

evidence.title: Amid NEET debate, TN CM Stalin calls for moving education to State List
evidence.snippet: CM Stalin has repeatedly called for education to be moved from the Concurrent List to the State List to allow states more autonomy, specifically to abolish exams like NEET.

plan_existence.notes: Education and Medicine were moved from the State List to the Concurrent List via the 42nd Amendment in 1976. While the demand is a long-standing policy stance of TN parties, no such transfer has occurred.

legal.comments: Requires a Constitutional Amendment under Article 368, necessitating a two-thirds majority in both Houses of Parliament and ratification by at least half of the State Legislatures.

administrative.comments: Transferring these departments would require massive restructuring of central schemes (like Samagra Shiksha or NHM) and regulatory bodies (UGC, NMC).

timeline.comments: Highly unlikely to be achieved within a 5-year term given the requirement for national consensus and central government approval.

political.comments: Strong support within Tamil Nadu, but likely to face significant opposition from the Union Government and other states that benefit from central standards.

feasibility.overall_comments: This is a high-level political demand with extremely low legal and timeline feasibility as it depends entirely on the Union Government's willingness to relinquish power.
key_risks: Constitutional amendment hurdles
Central government resistance
Potential loss of central funding for these sectors

evidence.title: 16th Finance Commission: Why the north-south tax row has shifted
evidence.snippet: The 16th Finance Commission (report Feb 2026) refused to include cesses and surcharges in the divisible pool, stating the Constitution does not permit it under the current scheme.

plan_existence.notes: Under Article 270 of the Constitution, cesses and surcharges are excluded from the divisible pool. The 16th Finance Commission recently rejected the demand from Tamil Nadu and 17 other states to include them.

fiscal.comments: While the state would gain significant revenue (estimated at 15% of gross tax revenue), the fiscal impact on the Union would be a corresponding loss, which they have already termed 'unsustainable'.

legal.comments: Requires an amendment to Article 270 of the Constitution, which is a central prerogative.

administrative.comments: Technically simple to implement if the law changes, as the tax collection mechanism already exists.

timeline.comments: The 16th Finance Commission has already set the formula for 2026-2031, making a change during this term highly unlikely.

political.comments: Strong collective bargaining power as 18 states have made similar demands, but the Union Government remains the final arbiter.

feasibility.overall_comments: A critical fiscal demand that faces a dead-end for the 2026-2031 period following the 16th Finance Commission's recent refusal to alter the divisible pool structure.
key_risks: Union Government's fiscal deficit concerns
Constitutional barriers
Fixed 5-year cycle of Finance Commission recommendations

evidence.title: Directorate of Vigilance and Anti-Corruption, Tamil Nadu
evidence.snippet: The DVAC is the specialized agency in Tamil Nadu to investigate corruption charges against public servants.

plan_existence.notes: While DVAC and Lokayukta exist, the promise specifically targets the 'past 5 years' and mentions reviewing complaints submitted to the Governor, which implies a specific political/legal probe rather than routine enforcement.

legal.comments: Prosecuting ministers requires sanction from the Governor under Section 19 of the Prevention of Corruption Act; legal challenges regarding 'political vendetta' are common.

administrative.comments: DVAC has the machinery, but large-scale retrospective probes across all departments would require significant manpower and specialized forensic auditors.

timeline.comments: Corruption trials in India often take decades; completing 'severe action' within a 5-year term is highly unlikely given judicial backlogs.

political.comments: High risk of being perceived as political vendetta, which can lead to legislative disruptions and retaliatory legal actions.

feasibility.overall_comments: The promise faces significant legal and procedural hurdles. While the intent aligns with existing laws, the specific focus on a 5-year window and Governor-submitted complaints suggests a politically charged process that often stalls in courts.

Refusal of prosecution sanction by the Governor

Difficulty in proving 'deep-rooted' corruption in court

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu Land Records e-Services
evidence.snippet: The Tamil Nilam portal provides computerized access to Patta/Chitta records across the state.

plan_existence.notes: The state is already computerizing land records. However, 'M.G.R. Pattas' (assigned lands) in tribal areas like Kalvarayan Hills often face specific hurdles due to forest land classifications and lack of updated surveys.

legal.comments: Issuing pattas in hilly/tribal areas often involves the Forest Conservation Act and the Scheduled Tribes and Other Traditional Forest Dwellers (Recognition of Forest Rights) Act, which require central clearances in some cases.

administrative.comments: The Revenue Department has the mechanism for computerization (Tamil Nilam), but physical verification in remote hilly terrains like Kalvarayan is labor-intensive.

timeline.comments: Surveying and computerizing specific blocks in two districts can be achieved within 2-3 years.

political.comments: Strong local political support as it addresses long-standing land tenure security issues for tribal communities.

feasibility.overall_comments: This is a focused, feasible administrative task that addresses a specific regional grievance. The main challenge is reconciling revenue records with forest department boundaries.
key_risks: Boundary disputes between Revenue and Forest departments
Incomplete historical records of MGR-era assignments
Resistance from forest conservation groups

existing_scheme_names: Hindu Religious and Charitable Endowments (HR&CE) Land Management

evidence.title: TN HR&CE Department - Land Management
evidence.snippet: The HR&CE department manages temple lands and has been involved in identifying and retrieving encroached or disputed Inam lands.

plan_existence.notes: Temple Inam land disputes are a long-standing issue in Tamil Nadu. While the HR&CE department has existing protocols, the promise to form a specific committee for Karur suggests a localized intervention to resolve specific settlement grievances.

legal.comments: Temple lands are governed by specific religious endowment laws and Supreme Court rulings that restrict the alienation of temple property, making 'solutions' like transferring ownership very difficult.

administrative.comments: Forming a committee is easy, but implementing its findings often requires legislative changes or lengthy court approvals.

timeline.comments: A committee can study the issue in 12 months, but legal resolution of land titles usually exceeds a 5-year term.

political.comments: Sensitive issue; any move to regularize temple land for residents may face opposition from religious groups or legal challenges.

feasibility.overall_comments: The promise to 'conduct a study' is feasible, but 'finding a solution' is legally fraught due to the protected status of temple properties. It is a common campaign promise that often results in protracted litigation.
key_risks: Supreme Court restrictions on temple land alienation
Opposition from religious organizations
Complexity of historical Inam title deeds

existing_scheme_names: Regularization of Encroachments on Unobjectionable Government Poramboke Lands

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu Revenue and Disaster Management Department - Land Administration
evidence.snippet: The department facilitates the assignment of house site pattas to landless poor and the regularization of residential encroachments in unobjectionable poramboke lands.

evidence.title: G.O. (Ms) No. 318 Revenue and Disaster Management - Regularisation
evidence.snippet: Government order issued in 2019 to regularize residential encroachments in unobjectionable poramboke lands for those residing for over 5 years.

plan_existence.notes: The issuance of pattas for long-term residents on unobjectionable government land is a standard administrative procedure in Tamil Nadu, governed by various Government Orders (G.O.s) and the Revenue Standing Orders. The current DMK government and previous AIADMK governments have both implemented similar schemes, often through special drives.

fiscal.comments: The promise specifies that residents will pay an 'amount fixed by the government,' making the scheme potentially revenue-neutral or revenue-generating for the state treasury, covering administrative and survey costs.

legal.comments: The state has clear legal authority under the Tamil Nadu Board of Revenue Standing Orders (BSO) to alienate 'unobjectionable' land. Legal risks are low as it excludes protected lands like water bodies or forests.

administrative.comments: Implementation requires extensive field surveys and verification by the Revenue Department. While the mechanism exists, it is often slowed by bureaucratic backlog and the need for precise land classification.

timeline.comments: The process can be executed within a 5-year term through dedicated 'Pattas for All' camps and digital land records integration.

political.comments: Land ownership is a high-impact political issue in Tamil Nadu with broad public support and minimal opposition for 'unobjectionable' land settlements.

feasibility.overall_comments: This is a highly feasible promise that continues an established administrative practice. It leverages existing legal frameworks and includes a payment model that mitigates fiscal strain. The primary challenge remains administrative efficiency and preventing corruption at the local level.
key_risks: Corruption in the verification of residence duration
Disputes over land classification as 'unobjectionable'
Administrative delays in the survey and issuance process

points.text: Considering the cost of paddy production and for the welfare of farmers, steps will be taken to fix the Minimum Support Price (MSP) for paddy at Rs. 3,500/- per quintal.

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu fixes MSP for paddy; procurement to begin September 1
evidence.snippet: The effective procurement rates stand at Rs 2,545 per quintal for Grade 'A' and Rs 2,500 per quintal for the common variety, including a state incentive of Rs 156 and Rs 131 respectively.

evidence.title: Effect minimum support price hike sans delay, TN delta farmers urge parties
evidence.snippet: The AIADMK, in its manifesto, has promised to fix MSP for paddy at Rs 3,500 a quintal and sugarcane at Rs 4,500 a quintal.

plan_existence.notes: The Government of India fixes the national MSP, while the Tamil Nadu government provides an additional state incentive. As of the 2025-26 season, the total payout is approximately Rs 2,500-2,545. The promise to 'fix' it at Rs 3,500 represents a nearly 40% increase in the total procurement price, which would be achieved by significantly increasing the state-funded incentive component.

fiscal.comments: While annual procurement volumes are grounded (approx. 42-47 lakh metric tonnes), no specific total budget outlay for this price hike is provided in the manifesto or cited sources. However, increasing the payout by ~Rs 1,000 per quintal across 4.2 million tonnes suggests a multi-thousand crore annual commitment.

legal.comments: State governments have established legal authority to provide 'State Advisory Prices' or additional incentives/bonuses over and above the Central MSP.

administrative.comments: The Tamil Nadu Civil Supplies Corporation (TNCSC) operates a robust network of over 3,000 Direct Procurement Centres (DPCs) and an established digital payment system for direct benefit transfer to farmers.

timeline.comments: The price hike can be implemented immediately at the start of the next procurement season (typically September 1) via a Government Order.

political.comments: There is strong bipartisan support for increasing farmer income; both major Dravidian parties have included similar hikes in their 2026 platforms, making it highly popular and politically safe.

feasibility.overall_comments: The promise is highly feasible from administrative, legal, and political perspectives. The primary challenge is the significant fiscal burden it places on the state exchequer to fund the gap between the Central MSP and the promised Rs 3,500 target.
key_risks: Significant increase in state fiscal deficit
Potential for food price inflation if market prices follow procurement rates
Dependence on Central Government MSP revisions to mitigate state incentive costs

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu: Minister announces special incentive of Rs 349 per MT for sugarcane
evidence.snippet: Agriculture Minister MRK Panneerselvam announced a special incentive of Rs 349 per Metric Tonne for the 2024-25 season, benefiting 1.30 lakh farmers with an allocation of Rs 297 crore.

evidence.title: Cane farmers press TN government to hike incentive
evidence.snippet: For the 2025-26 season, the FRP is Rs 3,550/tonne at a basic recovery rate of 10.25%. Farmers are demanding a realized price of Rs 4,000 to Rs 4,500 per tonne.

plan_existence.notes: The Central Government fixes the FRP (Fair and Remunerative Price), while the Tamil Nadu government currently provides a 'Special Incentive' to bridge the gap. The promise to fix the price at Rs 4,500 is an expansion of this existing incentive mechanism to meet long-standing farmer demands.

fiscal.comments: Current incentives of Rs 349/tonne cost the state ~Rs 297 crore. Increasing the total payout to Rs 4,500 (an additional ~Rs 600-1,000 over current levels) would likely require an annual outlay of Rs 700-1,000 crore, which is manageable within the state's agriculture budget.

legal.comments: The Supreme Court (2020) and Madras High Court (2023) have upheld the authority of state governments to provide State Advised Prices (SAP) or incentives above the Central FRP.

administrative.comments: The mechanism for Direct Benefit Transfer (DBT) to registered sugarcane farmers through sugar mills is already well-established in Tamil Nadu.

timeline.comments: This can be implemented immediately in the first crushing season following the election through a budget notification.

political.comments: High political feasibility as it addresses a core demand of the influential farming lobby and matches similar promises made by rivals DMK and TVK.

feasibility.overall_comments: The promise is highly feasible as it leverages existing administrative machinery and falls within reasonable fiscal limits. It is a standard price-support expansion that has clear legal backing and strong political necessity.
key_risks: Fiscal strain if sugarcane production/crushing volumes increase significantly
Fluctuations in sugar recovery rates affecting the base Central FRP calculation
Potential pressure on private sugar mills if the state mandates they share the cost

Tamil Nadu Small Farmers Agribusiness Consortium (TNSFAC)

evidence.title: Agriculture Budget 2024-2025 - TNAU Agritech Portal
evidence.snippet: The Government of Tamil Nadu implements the State Agriculture Development Scheme to benefit farmers and Farmer Producer Organizations (FPOs) with subsidies and infrastructure support.

evidence.title: State Level Consortium of Farmer Producer Organizations - SFAC
evidence.snippet: Initiatives have been taken to establish State Level Federations of FPOs to create an umbrella support for member FPOs in states including Tamil Nadu.

plan_existence.notes: FPO formation and financial assistance already exist under both Central (PM-FPO) and State (TNSFAC) schemes. A state-level consortium (TN Consortium of FPCL) already exists. The promise to 'get GST exemption' is a policy advocacy point as GST is governed by the GST Council, not the state alone.

fiscal.comments: Tamil Nadu already allocates significant funds (e.g., Rs. 45 crore in 2024-25 for agri development) for FPO support. However, the fiscal impact of a 5-year GST exemption depends on GST Council approval and would result in revenue loss for both state and center.

legal.comments: The state government cannot unilaterally grant GST exemptions. Under Article 279A of the Constitution, only the GST Council can recommend exemptions. The promise correctly phrases this as 'steps will be taken to get exemption'.

administrative.comments: Existing institutional frameworks like TNSFAC and NABARD are well-equipped to handle the federation and strengthening of FPOs.

timeline.comments: Federating existing FPCs and providing financial assistance can be achieved within a 5-year term.

political.comments: Strengthening farmer collectives is a popular policy with high public reception and minimal opposition risk.

feasibility.overall_comments: The promise is largely an expansion of existing state and central schemes. The most significant hurdle is the GST exemption, which is outside the state's direct legal jurisdiction and requires federal consensus.
key_risks: Dependency on GST Council for tax exemptions
Operational sustainability of FPOs after financial assistance ends
Inter-departmental coordination for state-level federation

evidence.title: Agricultural Schemes In India & Tamil Nadu: A Comprehensive Guide - Farmonaut
evidence.snippet: PM-KISAN provides direct income support of ₹6,000/year to small and marginal farmers. Tamil Nadu also implements various input-specific subsidies for micro-irrigation and organic farming.

evidence.title: Farming Made Easier: TN Govt to Give Rs 15,000 Subsidy to Farmers
evidence.snippet: The Tamil Nadu government provides a maximum subsidy of Rs 15,000 for obtaining export certifications and for purchasing electric motor pump sets, but not as a general per-hectare production subsidy for inputs.

plan_existence.notes: While PM-KISAN (Central) provides Rs. 6,000 annually and TN has specific subsidies for machinery or certifications (Rs. 15,000), a dedicated 'production subsidy' of Rs. 15,000 per hectare for seeds and fertilizers is a new and significantly higher financial commitment than current direct cash transfers.

fiscal.comments: Tamil Nadu has approximately 79 lakh operational holdings, of which over 90% are small and marginal. Providing Rs. 15,000 per hectare would require an annual outlay likely exceeding Rs. 5,000-7,000 crore, placing a heavy burden on the state exchequer.

legal.comments: Agriculture is a state subject; the state government has full legal authority to provide production subsidies.

administrative.comments: The existing GEMS/Agrisnet and PM-KISAN databases for small/marginal farmers make identification and Direct Benefit Transfer (DBT) administratively feasible.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented within the first year of a new term through budget allocation.

political.comments: Highly popular among the rural electorate; aligns with the demands of major farmer unions.

feasibility.overall_comments: The scheme is administratively ready due to existing farmer databases but faces significant fiscal hurdles. It represents a major escalation in direct income support compared to the current PM-KISAN levels.
key_risks: High fiscal deficit impact
Verification of actual cultivation vs. land ownership
Potential for leakage if not strictly linked to Aadhaar-seeded land records

evidence.title: T.N. election: AIADMK's promise of crop loan waiver raises questions of feasibility
evidence.snippet: The AIADMK's scheme covers crop loans obtained by farmers from primary agricultural credit cooperative societies (PACCS). The share of loans taken through PACCS constitutes about 5% to 6% of total crop loans in the state.

plan_existence.notes: Loan waivers are periodic in Tamil Nadu. The DMK government implemented a Rs. 12,110 crore waiver in 2021. This promise seeks to repeat the cycle for loans accrued since the last waiver.

fiscal.comments: The 2021 waiver cost the state over Rs. 12,000 crore. A fresh waiver would significantly increase the state's debt-to-GSDP ratio, which is already under pressure.

legal.comments: The state has authority over cooperative societies, but the RBI frequently cautions against the moral hazard and impact on credit discipline.

administrative.comments: PACCS are state-controlled and increasingly digitized, making the identification of beneficiaries and loan amounts straightforward.

timeline.comments: Can be executed immediately via a government order and budgetary provision.

political.comments: Extremely high political salience; often a 'deal-breaker' promise in Tamil Nadu elections.

feasibility.overall_comments: Technically simple to implement but fiscally damaging. It targets a small segment of total agri-debt (PACCS) but carries massive political weight.
key_risks: Erosion of credit culture/moral hazard
Increased fiscal deficit
Exclusion of farmers who borrow from commercial banks

evidence.title: What is C2+50 Formula of MSP, How this can change life of farmers?
evidence.snippet: The Swaminathan Commission proposed the C2+50% formula. Currently, the Central government uses A2+FL+50%, which is lower. Farmers are demanding a legal guarantee for C2+50%.

evidence.title: No crop loan waiver, key demands not met, say farmers in delta
evidence.snippet: Farmer leaders in TN have expressed disappointment over the failure to ensure MSP based on the M.S. Swaminathan formula.

plan_existence.notes: While MSP exists, it is determined by the Center using the A2+FL formula. Tamil Nadu provides a small additional incentive for paddy. Implementing C2+50% for all grains, pulses, and oilseeds at the state level would be a first in India.

fiscal.comments: Implementing C2+50% would require the state to pay the massive difference between the Central MSP and the Swaminathan rate. For paddy alone, this could cost several thousand crores; extending it to pulses and oilseeds without central support is likely fiscally unsustainable.

legal.comments: While the state can provide 'incentives,' a formal 'fixing' of MSP often conflicts with Central procurement policies and WTO regulations on subsidies.

administrative.comments: Procurement infrastructure for paddy (TNCSC) is robust, but the state lacks the storage and logistics to procure all pulses and oilseeds at scale.

timeline.comments: Setting up the price commission and procurement centers for non-paddy crops would take 2-3 years.

political.comments: A core demand of the national and state farmer movements; high political reward.

feasibility.overall_comments: A high-stakes promise that is fiscally perilous. The state would essentially be subsidizing the gap between Central policy and the Swaminathan recommendations for a wide range of crops.
key_risks: Extreme fiscal strain
Lack of storage infrastructure for pulses/oilseeds
Conflict with Central government's CACP pricing

plan_existence.notes: Extensive central and state subsidies already exist for private and cooperative entities to build storage infrastructure. The promise suggests an increase in the scale or accessibility of these subsidies.

legal.comments: Standard infrastructure development and subsidy provision; no legal constraints.

administrative.comments: Requires efficient processing of subsidy applications and monitoring of construction quality.

timeline.comments: Large-scale infrastructure projects (cold storage/warehouses) typically take 18-36 months to become operational.

political.comments: Reducing post-harvest loss is a non-controversial and highly welcomed policy goal.

feasibility.overall_comments: The promise is highly feasible by leveraging the Government of India's AIF and PMKSY schemes. The state's role would be to provide additional top-up subsidies or facilitate land and power connections.
key_risks: High electricity costs for cold storage
Low capacity utilization in off-seasons
Land acquisition challenges for large-scale units

evidence.title: Tamil Nadu's Kalaignar Kanavu Illam housing scheme
evidence.snippet: The primary objective of the scheme is to provide free permanent concrete houses to economically weaker families living in huts or dilapidated houses in rural areas. Land will be provided to beneficiaries who do not own land.

evidence.title: WELFARE OF LANDLESS AGRICULTURAL LABOURERS
evidence.snippet: A statement showing the State-wise data of landless agricultural workers as per Census 2011 shows Tamil Nadu has 9,606,547 such laborers.

plan_existence.notes: While the 2011 Census provides data, a dedicated current census for landless laborers is not active. Housing is already addressed via the 'Kalaignar Kanavu Illam' scheme launched in 2024, which specifically targets hut-dwellers and provides land pattas to the landless.

fiscal.comments: The existing Kalaignar Kanavu Illam scheme already has an outlay of Rs 3,500 crore for the first phase (1 lakh houses). Scaling this to all 9.6 million laborers would require hundreds of thousands of crores, which is fiscally daunting.

legal.comments: The state has the authority to conduct a census and implement welfare schemes under the Directive Principles of State Policy.

administrative.comments: Conducting a state-wide census of a specific occupational group is administratively intensive but feasible through the revenue department and local panchayats.

timeline.comments: A census can be completed in 1-2 years, but providing housing and employment to millions of laborers is a multi-decade task.

political.comments: High political appeal as landless laborers constitute a significant vote bank and face extreme vulnerability.

feasibility.overall_comments: The census and basic assistance are highly feasible, but the promise of housing for all landless laborers is a massive fiscal commitment that duplicates and vastly exceeds the scale of current state housing missions.
key_risks: Extreme fiscal burden for universal housing
Identification errors in census
Land availability for landless beneficiaries

evidence.title: Farmers urge Tamil Nadu government to provide farm drones at affordable rent
evidence.snippet: Despite the agricultural engineering department owning and renting out various farm implements, it currently has no drones. The government currently offers a 50% subsidy to individual farmers purchasing drones.

plan_existence.notes: While subsidies for purchasing drones exist (up to 50-80%), the Agricultural Engineering Department does not currently maintain a fleet for direct rental. Some cooperatives (e.g., in Karur) have started this on a pilot basis, but it is not a statewide government-run rental service.

fiscal.comments: Procuring drones for rental is less expensive than universal subsidies for individual ownership. A drone costs roughly Rs 5-10 lakh; equipping every block would cost a few hundred crores.

legal.comments: Complies with DGCA Drone Rules 2021; no legal barriers to state-owned rental services.

administrative.comments: Requires skilled pilots and maintenance staff within the Ag Engineering Dept, which currently lacks this capacity.

timeline.comments: Procurement and training can be completed within 2-3 years.

political.comments: Strongly supported by farmers facing labor shortages and high manual spraying costs.

feasibility.overall_comments: A highly practical solution to labor shortages that shifts the burden from farmer capital expenditure to a service-based model, though it requires building a technical maintenance wing in the government.
key_risks: High maintenance costs of drone fleets
Shortage of certified drone pilots
Battery life and charging infrastructure in rural areas

evidence.title: Integrated Wasteland Development Programme - TNAU
evidence.snippet: The IWDP has been under implementation in Tamil Nadu since 1993-94 to develop non-forest wastelands on the principles of watershed development.

plan_existence.notes: Wasteland development and farm forestry are long-standing programs in Tamil Nadu. The promise to make it a 'separate movement' suggests a revival or rebranding of the Comprehensive Wasteland Development Programme (CWDP) of the early 2000s.

fiscal.comments: Funding can be leveraged from existing central schemes like MGNREGS and the Green India Mission. State outlays for such programs are typically manageable (Rs 50-100 crore annually).

legal.comments: No legal hurdles; involves land use optimization on private and common lands.

administrative.comments: The Tamil Nadu Watershed Development Agency (TAWDEVA) and the Forest Department have the existing machinery to implement this.

timeline.comments: Can be launched immediately as it builds on existing departmental structures.

political.comments: Generally popular as it increases land value and provides additional income through timber/fodder.

feasibility.overall_comments: This is a low-risk, high-benefit environmental and agricultural promise that largely continues existing state priorities under a more focused banner.

Water availability for initial growth
Long gestation period for forestry returns

evidence.title: TN's organic policy prepares field for safe food, soil health

timeline.comments: Implementation is already underway under the 2023 policy framework.

existing_scheme_names: Chief Minister's Solar Powered Pump-set Scheme

evidence.title: Chief Minister's Solar Powered Pump-set Scheme - Tamil Nadu
evidence.snippet: The scheme provides off-grid standalone solar pumping systems with subsidies ranging from 60% to 80% depending on the category of farmers.

evidence.title: PM-KUSUM Benefits Reach Over 21.77 Lakh Farmers
evidence.snippet: PM-KUSUM Component B provides standalone solar pumps with 60% subsidy (30% Central + 30% State).

plan_existence.notes: Existing schemes provide 60-80% subsidy for solar pumps. The promise to provide a 100% subsidy is an amendment to increase the financial support to cover the full cost for farmers.

fiscal.comments: Moving from 60-80% to 100% subsidy imposes a significant additional burden on the state exchequer, especially as solar pump sets cost between Rs. 2.5 lakh to Rs. 5 lakh depending on HP.

legal.comments: No legal hurdles; the state has the authority to increase subsidy percentages for welfare schemes.

administrative.comments: The Agricultural Engineering Department and TEDA already have the infrastructure to distribute solar pumps; scaling to 100% subsidy is administratively simple.

timeline.comments: Can be implemented within a 5-year term through phased budgetary allocations.

political.comments: Highly popular among farmers, particularly those waiting for grid connections (TANGEDCO backlog).

feasibility.overall_comments: The plan is highly feasible administratively but requires substantial fiscal commitment to bridge the 20-40% gap currently paid by farmers or the Centre.
key_risks: High fiscal outlay
Potential for misuse/resale of fully subsidized equipment
Dependence on Central component of PM-KUSUM

evidence.title: TN raises compensation for landless agricultural workers

Budgetary pressure if death tolls from these causes rise

existing_scheme_names: Simplified Alluvial Soil and Clay Collection Norms

evidence.title: Now, farmers, pottery workers in TN can take alluvial soil, clay free of cost
evidence.snippet: Chief Minister MK Stalin on Wednesday allowed farmers and pottery workers to take alluvial soil (vandalmann) and clay (kaliman) from water sources like irrigation tanks, ponds, canals, lakes, etc. free of cost.

evidence.title: T.N. CM issues orders for farmers, potters to take silt, clay from waterbodies for free
evidence.snippet: The Natural Resources Department had, last month, issued orders to simplify the process by delegating powers vested with District Collector to the Tahsildars of concerned jurisdictions.

plan_existence.notes: The Tamil Nadu government already issued a Government Order in June 2024 simplifying the process for farmers and potters to collect silt and clay. The power to grant permission was delegated from Collectors to Tahsildars to reduce 'hindrance'.

fiscal.comments: The scheme is already operational and involves no direct cash outflow; it actually aids in desilting water bodies, which is a maintenance requirement.

legal.comments: Amendments to the Tamil Nadu Minor Mineral Concession Rules have already been made to facilitate this.

administrative.comments: An online portal for applications and delegation to the Tahsildar level is already in place.

timeline.comments: Already implemented and can be continued indefinitely.

political.comments: Highly popular among the agrarian and artisan communities with no significant opposition.

feasibility.overall_comments: This promise is already a reality in Tamil Nadu. It is highly feasible because the administrative framework and legal amendments were completed in 2024.
key_risks: Illegal sand mining under the guise of silt collection
Potential damage to tank bunds if excavation is not monitored
Corruption at the local Tahsildar/VAO level for permissions

existing_scheme_names: PVC Pipe laying & Community Bore wells (AED)

evidence.title: Government Schemes - TNAU Agritech Portal
evidence.snippet: PVC Pipe laying & Community Bore wells: 100% grant to farmers. However, 10% beneficiary contribution is collected for maintenance.

plan_existence.notes: The Agricultural Engineering Department (AED) already provides 100% grants for community borewells for irrigation. Drinking water is largely covered under the Jal Jeevan Mission and TWAD Board schemes.

fiscal.comments: Significant capital expenditure required for drilling and pump installation, though partially covered by existing central/state funds.

legal.comments: Groundwater extraction is strictly regulated; approximately 31% of TN blocks are classified as over-exploited, making new borewells legally difficult in those areas.

administrative.comments: Local bodies and the TWAD board have the technical capacity, but coordination between irrigation and drinking water departments is needed.

timeline.comments: Borewells can be sunk quickly, but the 'feasible districts' caveat suggests a phased rollout over 5 years.

political.comments: Generally well-received, though conflicts can arise between agricultural and domestic water users over shared sources.

feasibility.overall_comments: The plan is feasible but faces significant environmental and legal hurdles due to Tamil Nadu's declining groundwater levels. It expands on existing AED and JJM frameworks.
key_risks: Rapid depletion of groundwater in critical blocks
Legal restrictions on new borewells in over-exploited zones
Maintenance failures of community-owned infrastructure

plan_existence.notes: Tamil Nadu currently provides 12-16 hours of 3-phase power. While there is a 2021 mandate for 24-hour supply, it is being implemented slowly due to grid constraints and high demand.

fiscal.comments: TANGEDCO is under massive debt (over ₹1.5 lakh crore). Providing 24/7 free 3-phase power would exponentially increase the subsidy burden on the state exchequer.

legal.comments: TNERC has already directed this, so there are no legal hurdles, only financial and technical ones.

administrative.comments: Current transmission lines and transformers are frequently overloaded. Upgrading the entire rural grid to handle 24/7 3-phase load is a massive technical challenge.

timeline.comments: Full statewide implementation is unlikely within 5 years given the current pace of feeder separation and grid upgrades.

political.comments: Extremely high political demand; a core promise for any party seeking the rural vote.

feasibility.overall_comments: While politically essential, the promise is fiscally and technically difficult. The state is currently at 16 hours; reaching 24 hours requires billions in grid investment and power procurement.
key_risks: Bankruptcy of TANGEDCO due to unpaid subsidies
Frequent transformer failures and grid instability
Increased groundwater depletion due to unrestricted pumping

plan_existence.notes: Kudimaramathu was a flagship scheme of the previous AIADMK government. fiscal.comments: Previous iterations had outlays between Rs 100 crore to Rs 500 crore annually, which is manageable within the state budget.

legal.comments: No legal hurdles; the state has full authority over minor irrigation and local water bodies.

administrative.comments: The Water Resources Department (WRD) has established protocols for this scheme, though monitoring community participation to prevent contractor-driven work is a known challenge.

Political branding conflicts

existing_scheme_names: Tamil Nadu Policy on Invasive Plants and Ecological Restoration (TNPIPER)

evidence.title: Madras HC declares war on karuvelam - New Indian Express
evidence.snippet: In March 2026, the Madras High Court ordered the state to implement project 'Sezhumai Karuvoolam' for the eradication of Seemai Karuvelam from private and public lands.

evidence.title: 517 villages in Tamil Nadu are 'seemai karuvelam'-free - The Hindu
evidence.snippet: The TN government informed the court that 517 villages have become free of the invasive species as of late 2025.

plan_existence.notes: Eradication of Seemai Karuvelam is a long-standing judicial mandate in Tamil Nadu. The state already has a dedicated policy (TNPIPER 2022) and a court-monitored project (Sezhumai Karuvoolam) to remove these trees from water bodies and riverbeds.

fiscal.comments: High costs for mechanical uprooting; however, the court has suggested auctioning the wood to fund the removal, making it partially self-sustaining.

legal.comments: Strong legal backing from the Madras High Court, which has issued 34 directions for total eradication.

administrative.comments: Extremely difficult to execute completely due to the species' rapid regrowth from deep roots and seed banks, requiring multi-year follow-up.

timeline.comments: Complete removal statewide is unlikely in 5 years given the scale, but significant progress in water bodies is possible.

political.comments: Broad public and political support as the species is widely recognized as a threat to groundwater.

feasibility.overall_comments: Feasible as a continuation of existing efforts, but 'complete removal' is an ecological challenge that has eluded the state for over a decade despite court orders.
key_risks: Rapid regrowth from soil seed banks
Lack of coordination between different land-owning departments (PWD, Forest, Revenue)

evidence.title: Farmers urge State Government to pursue Anamalayar and Nallar sub-projects - The Hindu
evidence.snippet: The PAP agreement of 1958 provided for the Anamalayar project, but it remains unfulfilled pending Kerala's consent and the construction of the Idamalayar dam (completed in 1985).

evidence.title: TN govt to renovate Parambikulam Aliyar Project - The Statesman
evidence.snippet: The Water Resources Department is preparing a DPR for PAP renovation at an estimated cost of Rs 4,000 crore (August 2024).

plan_existence.notes: The Parambikulam-Aliyar Project (PAP) is an existing inter-state system. The Anaimalaiyar and Nallar dams are long-pending components of the original 1958 agreement that require Kerala's concurrence for implementation. A DPR for renovation exists, but the new dams are stalled by inter-state disputes.

fiscal.comments: The renovation alone is estimated at Rs 4,000-4,500 crore. Constructing two new dams would require significantly higher outlays not yet fully budgeted.

legal.comments: Requires a new or renewed inter-state agreement with Kerala. Kerala has historically linked this to other water disputes (Mullaperiyar), making legal/diplomatic progress very slow.

administrative.comments: TN WRD has the technical capacity to build the dams, but cannot proceed without environmental clearances and inter-state permits.

timeline.comments: Unlikely to be completed in 5 years given the history of the dispute and the time required for dam construction.

political.comments: High political pressure in Western TN, but requires high-level political negotiation with the Kerala government, which is often at odds with TN on water sharing.

feasibility.overall_comments: While the formation of a committee is easy, the actual construction of the dams is highly infeasible within a 5-year term due to the complex inter-state legal and political deadlock with Kerala.
key_risks: Refusal of consent by the Kerala Government
Environmental and forest clearance hurdles in the Western Ghats
Inter-state litigation in the Supreme Court
